description: Diagnose the seeded voucher-payment dropoff. Input is the Anomaly object produced by seededToAnomaly(voucher-dropoff-w10-on) — same shape the production monitoring agent emits.

anomaly.impact: payment_value for payment_type:voucher fell ~95% in a sustained dropoff. Investigate the root cause.

golden_output.conclusion: The voucher payment_value collapsed ~93.7% starting the week of 2026-02-02 due to what appears to be a deliberate discontinuation or severe restriction of the voucher payment program. Both payment_value (BRL 32,567 avg/week → BRL 2,051 total across 17 weeks) and order_count (≈40 orders/week → 1–6 orders/week) dropped simultaneously in a sharp cliff pattern, while all other payment types (credit_card, boleto, debit_card) continued operating at normal or slightly increased volumes throughout the same period. The abruptness and isolation of the drop rule out both a data ingestion gap and a broad platform or category-driven event.
evidence: Voucher payment_value fell from a baseline avg of BRL 32,567,478/week to a total anomaly-window value of BRL 2,051,069 across ~17 weeks (pct_change = -93.7%), per get_anomaly_context.

Voucher order_count collapsed from ~39–91 orders/week during Dec 2025–Jan 2026 to 1–6 orders/week from 2026-02-02 onward — confirming the drop is in transaction volume, not just recorded payment value.
The collapse is a sudden cliff at the week of 2026-02-02: voucher payment_value went from BRL 2,458,432 (week of 2026-01-26) to BRL 59,499 (week of 2026-02-02), a >97% single-week drop.

Credit_card payment_value remained healthy (e.g. BRL 11.9M → 15.1M across early Feb weeks); boleto similarly stable at BRL 4–6M/week; debit_card showed a slight uptick (+17.9%). The platform was fully operational — ruling out a broad data ingestion or platform outage.
Related segments (boleto -16.8%, credit_card -10.3%) show only minor seasonal softening, in stark contrast to voucher's -93.7% — the drop is uniquely isolated to the voucher payment type.

Sample orders in the anomaly window confirm voucher orders did continue to be placed and delivered (e.g. ord_0006xt delivered 2026-02-17, ord_0006fs shipped 2026-02-24), but at drastically reduced frequency, consistent with a program wind-down rather than a technical recording failure.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Pipeline/tracking gap — voucher payment records stopped being ingested, causing an artificial drop in metrics without a real business change.
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: All other payment types (credit_card, boleto, debit_card) show continuous, healthy volumes throughout the anomaly window with no gaps. Sample orders in the anomaly window confirm real voucher orders were placed and recorded, just far fewer of them. A pipeline gap would affect all payment types or leave zero records, neither of which occurred.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Voucher program discontinuation — the business stopped issuing or broadly accepting vouchers as a payment method starting around 2026-02-02, causing genuine transaction volume collapse isolated to this payment type.
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: Both payment_value and order_count dropped simultaneously in a sharp single-week cliff (>97% week-over-week) starting precisely 2026-02-02, and the level never recovered across 17 subsequent weeks. All other payment types remained unaffected. The abruptness, isolation, and sustained nature of the drop (not a gradual slide) are hallmarks of a deliberate policy or program change rather than organic demand erosion.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Category or regional collapse driving voucher usage — a key product category or geographic region that heavily relied on vouchers experienced a sharp decline, indirectly pulling voucher payment_value down.
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: The drop in voucher orders is so extreme (from 40+/week to 1–6/week) and so abrupt that a category or regional demand shift cannot explain it — such shifts are typically gradual and partial. Moreover, the overall platform order volume remained healthy, meaning demand existed but customers were simply not paying with vouchers. The root cause is upstream (voucher issuance/acceptance) not downstream (category demand).

affectedCustomers.segmentDescription: Total orders associated with the voucher payment type across the full analysis window (2025-12-01 to 2026-06-01), per totalCount returned by get_metric_timeseries. The anomaly period (2026-02-02 to 2026-06-01) accounts for only ~69 of these orders vs ~394 in the baseline period, representing a ~83% reduction in affected customer transactions.

scoring_config.notes: PR E established this anomaly is the strongest performer (10/10 pass, 100%). Regression check anchors on the supported hypothesis (program suspension / voucher policy change).
